title: Iteration, mutation and immutability

publication.changeSummary: Language-specific source-linked draft lesson for Iteration, mutation and immutability; independent technical, source, pedagogy, accessibility, and runnable-example review remains required. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

sources.locator: Learn Java; accessed 2026-07; Generics and Collections Framework learning paths; atomic topic: Iteration, mutation and immutability
sources.claim: Collection traversal must follow the iterator and ownership contract: use an iterator's remove when supported, bulk operations, or a separate result instead of structurally mutating behind an active traversal.

canonicalExplanation.summary: Collection traversal must follow the iterator and ownership contract: use an iterator's remove when supported, bulk operations, or a separate result instead of structurally mutating behind an active traversal.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: An Iterator removes expired entries through iterator.remove while visiting, avoiding a structural change through the collection itself. Your goal is to repair an unsafe collection mutation and document ownership of the resulting collection. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Interfaces; Arrays and multidimensional arrays; List, Set, Map and Queue; Bounds and wildcards. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Language-specific operational model
sections.body: Collection traversal must follow the iterator and ownership contract: use an iterator's remove when supported, bulk operations, or a separate result instead of structurally mutating behind an active traversal. This model is applied through the declared Java: Beginner to Intermediate behavior rather than a language-neutral slogan.

sections.heading: Boundary and failure model
sections.body: Fail-fast behavior is best-effort rather than synchronization, unmodifiable views can reflect backing changes, immutable copies detach differently, and concurrent collections define separate consistency rules. The learner must reject this documented misconception: Removing directly from an ArrayList inside an enhanced-for loop is safe whenever the removed element is the current one.

includes: Traverse safely, avoid concurrent modification and use immutable views or copies intentionally.
Repair an unsafe collection mutation and document ownership of the resulting collection.
excludes: Fail-fast behavior is best-effort rather than synchronization, unmodifiable views can reflect backing changes, immutable copies detach differently, and concurrent collections define separate consistency rules.

outcomes: Repair an unsafe collection mutation and document ownership of the resulting collection.

examples.title: Iteration, mutation and immutability: direct language case
examples.situation: An Iterator removes expired entries through iterator.remove while visiting, avoiding a structural change through the collection itself.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Observe the concrete Java: Beginner to Intermediate state or tool result: An Iterator removes expired entries through iterator.remove while visiting, avoiding a structural change through the collection itself.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Verify the result with this correction: Mutate through the traversal mechanism or after traversal, and distinguish an unmodifiable view from an immutable detached copy.
examples.result: The example meets the atomic outcome because Mutate through the traversal mechanism or after traversal, and distinguish an unmodifiable view from an immutable detached copy.

examples.title: Iteration, mutation and immutability: boundary and transfer case
examples.situation: List.copyOf creates an unmodifiable snapshot, while Collections.unmodifiableList creates a view whose contents can still change through the backing list.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Name what changed from the direct case: List.copyOf creates an unmodifiable snapshot, while Collections.unmodifiableList creates a view whose contents can still change through the backing list.

Step 2 — Keep the language boundary explicit: Fail-fast behavior is best-effort rather than synchronization, unmodifiable views can reflect backing changes, immutable copies detach differently, and concurrent collections define separate consistency rules.
Step 3 — Reject the tempting but incorrect rule: Removing directly from an ArrayList inside an enhanced-for loop is safe whenever the removed element is the current one.
examples.result: This case demonstrates the declared outcome: Repair an unsafe collection mutation and document ownership of the resulting collection.

misconceptions.mistakenBelief: Removing directly from an ArrayList inside an enhanced-for loop is safe whenever the removed element is the current one.
misconceptions.correction: Mutate through the traversal mechanism or after traversal, and distinguish an unmodifiable view from an immutable detached copy.
misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Predict both examples and identify the first observable contradiction in this belief: Removing directly from an ArrayList inside an enhanced-for loop is safe whenever the removed element is the current one.
